macro_segments.title: Traveling Mourner and Limited Business Dealings

micro_segments.title: The mourner traveling from place to place and minimizing business
Ambiguity about limiting dealings versus rolling with the group

macro_segments.title: When Bed-Upturning Begins: Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua

micro_segments.title: When does the mourning status begin—when the dead leaves the house or when buried
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua dispute with the Rabban Gamliel example
Ruling follows the stringent view requiring burial before bed-upturning

macro_segments.title: Restoring Beds on Erev Shabbat and Sitting Restrictions

micro_segments.title: When beds can be restored on Erev Shabbat from Mincha time onward
Rav Bar Huna: one cannot sit on restored beds even if they are prepared for Shabbat

macro_segments.title: All Beds in the House Must Be Upturned, Not Just the Mourner's Own

micro_segments.title: All beds in the house are upturned, not just the mourner's personal bed
Beds used for storing vessels do not need to be upturned
The Dargash and whether it must be upturned

macro_segments.title: Definition and Treatment of the Dargash—A Leather Couch-Like Seat

micro_segments.title: Ulla: the Dargash is a couch of good luck; Rabba's challenge about its use
Rav Ashi's objection: if it's a luck-couch, why does it have straps
Ravin and the marketplace report: Dargash is a leather-seated frame with straps
Alternative explanation: weaving location distinguishes Dargash from normal beds
Halakha ruled like Rabban Shimon ben Gamliel to release the straps

macro_segments.title: Four-Poster Beds and Standing Them Upright Without Full Inversion

micro_segments.title: Beds with tall posts need only be stood upright, not inverted

macro_segments.title: Sitting or Sleeping on Upturned Beds and Other Furniture

micro_segments.title: If sleeping on chair or big couch, one must sit on upturned furniture to fulfill obligation
Rabbi Yochanan: failing to sit on upturned bed means not fulfilling bed-upturning obligation

macro_segments.title: Cleaning and Beautification of the Mourner's House

micro_segments.title: One may sweep, wash floors, and wash dishes in the mourner's house
One may not bring incense and aromatic spices to the mourner's house
Bar Kappara: no blessing on incense in mourner's house but blessing in comforters' house

macro_segments.title: Food Presentation to Mourners: From Fancy Platters to Simple Baskets

micro_segments.title: Food brought to mourner's house in simple baskets, not fancy platters

macro_segments.title: Funeral Restrictions During Chol HaMoed and for Talmidei Chachamim

micro_segments.title: Women's coffins not left in street due to respect, regardless of Chol HaMoed

macro_segments.title: Food Presentation to Mourners: From Fancy Platters to Simple Baskets (continued)

micro_segments.title: Originally rich brought silver/gold trays, poor brought wicker; reformed for equality

macro_segments.title: Drinking Vessels and Progressive Reforms for the Dignity of the Poor

micro_segments.title: Originally rich drank from clear glass, poor from colored; reformed for equality

macro_segments.title: Covering Faces of the Dead and Equalizing Funeral Practices

micro_segments.title: Originally faces of rich shown, poor covered due to famine-darkening; reformed to cover all
Originally rich removed in Dargash, poor in simple coffin; reformed for equality
Incense placed under all bodies for dignity of those with intestinal illness

macro_segments.title: Immersion of Vessels After Death of Women and Men with Ritual Impurity

micro_segments.title: Originally vessels of deceased Niddot immersed to spare living Niddot embarrassment
Similarly, vessels of deceased Zavim immersed for dignity of living Zavim

macro_segments.title: Simplification of Funeral Attire by Rabban Gamliel to Relieve Economic Burden

micro_segments.title: Originally funeral expenses were more burdensome than death itself; relatives abandoned bodies
Rabban Gamliel reformed: buried in simple linen garments, creating precedent for all
Rav Papa: nowadays even inexpensive cloak is acceptable for burial

macro_segments.title: Funeral Restrictions During Chol HaMoed and for Talmidei Chachamim (continued)

micro_segments.title: Do not leave bier in street on Chol HaMoed to avoid encouraging eulogies
Rav Papa: no Chol HaMoed restrictions for Talmid Chacham; Rav Kahana eulogized Rav Zvid from distance
Rav Pappi: day of hearing the death is considered presence of body for eulogizing

macro_segments.title: Physical Expressions of Mourning and Eulogies: Beating, Clapping, Stamping

micro_segments.title: Ulla: Eulogy of the heart involves beating breasts, clapping hands, and stamping feet
Those stamping feet should use shoes, not sandals, to avoid injury

macro_segments.title: When to Stop Offering Consolation: Mourner's Signal and Respect for Space

micro_segments.title: Once mourner nods head, consolers should not sit with him; he needs solitude
All must stand for Nasi except mourner and sick, who are exempt from such formalities
For everyone you say 'sit,' except mourner and sick, who needn't stand for Nasi

macro_segments.title: Feeding the Mourner: First Meal Must Come From Others

micro_segments.title: On first day, mourner cannot eat his own bread; must be fed by others
Source in Yechezkel: God told Yechezkel not to eat bread of people, showing mourner must be fed by others
Rabbah and Rav Yosef exchanged food with each other when both were mourners

macro_segments.title: Community Obligation: Work Cessation When Someone Dies in the City

micro_segments.title: If someone dies in city, all residents forbidden from work
Rav Hamnuna story: people doing work after death announcement; he threatened them with Nidduy
Explanation: when there is Chevra Kadisha society, work prohibition is lifted

macro_segments.title: Excessive Mourning as Sign of Secret Grief and the Woman Who Cried Too Much

micro_segments.title: One who grieves excessively over dead must be grieving over another death
Story of woman with seven sons: cried excessively over one death; all sons died
Rav Huna's warning and prophecy: if you don't listen, prepare shrouds for others

macro_segments.title: Prescribed Limits on Crying and Mourning Practices from Yirmiyahu's Prophecy

micro_segments.title: Yirmiyahu: do not cry excessively for dead; three days for weeping, seven for eulogizing
Thirty days for laundry and haircut; after that, God says don't have more compassion than Me

macro_segments.title: Appropriate Grief: Crying for Those Without Children and Chronic Sinners

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda: cry for one who dies without children; Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi only visits such mourners
Rav Huna: person who repeats sin comes to see sin as permitted, then refuses Teshuvah

macro_segments.title: Emotional and Psychological States of Mourners Across the Seven Days

micro_segments.title: First three days: mourner feels as if sword hovers between his sides
Days three to seven: sword is in the room but in the corner
After seven days: sword passes by in the street; ongoing sense of mortality

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua (beginning of mourning)
Mitah Meyuchedet Lekelim (bed for storing vessels)
Excessive mourning as sign of other grief
